title: Chapter 4: Under the Storm's Wing
paragraphs: The sky over the wastelands was covered with heavy, leaden clouds as the weary group of people finally reached the foot of the splintered dome of false hope—the old Observatory of Sivarus. The storm began with monstrous suddenness, raining down not ordinary rain, but corrosive acid mixed with gray radioactive ash. The drops left red, itchy burns on the skin.

Ravan heavily and wheezily inhaled air with torn lungs. The physical power that the former dentist was proud of was turning against him here—his massive body simply didn't have enough dirty oxygen. But he didn't complain. He gritted his teeth and literally dragged the stumbling Evgeniy on himself. Yuri nervously wiped his fogged glasses with a dirty sleeve, trying to see the road. Anar, whose usual impenetrable laziness had been replaced by animal concentration, walked as the rear guard, clutching a knife blade in his hand, ready to give his life to cover his friends' backs.

"Over here!" Alexander shouted over the deafening howl of the wind. He pointed with his hand to the dark, gaping opening of the entrance to the ruins like a mouth. "We'll wait out the storm inside. Fast!"
They tumbled into the dark hall littered with ancient fragments, breathing heavily and coughing from the ashy suspension... and immediately froze, paralyzed by primal horror.

In the center of the huge circular hall, slowly rising from his knees before some semi-erased mural on the wall, stood an unfathomable, gigantic silhouette. Hearing their footsteps, the stranger smoothly turned. The grinding of metal armor broke through the noise of the rain.

Instead of hair, a living, absolutely black flame blazed on the tall creature encased in dark armor, which seemed to absorb the meager light in the room. In his eyes—two endless wells of absolute emptiness—there was neither anticipation of a victim nor rage. Only bottomless, suffocating pain splashed in them, with which this monster seemed to have come to terms.
Alexander reacted on instincts. He raised his rusty crossbow, aiming it at the monster's chest.
"Not a step back, guys," Alexander said in a deceptively calm voice, though his heart was beating with the frequency of a drum roll.

Ravan let go of Evgeniy, stepped forward and stood like a wide wall between the elf and his friends. Anar silently slipped to the side, preparing to strike in a blind spot. Five ragged, exhausted people from their world lined up in a monolithic shield. There was no readiness to give up in their eyes. Only absolute, unconditional devotion to each other.

Avotu froze. In this rotten world, he was used to meeting only betrayal, fear, and animal egoism. What he saw in front of him broke all his expectations. These weak mortals, devoid of mana, were ready to throw themselves at a demon a thousand times superior to them, just to buy an extra minute of life for their friends.
The fire elf felt something stir deep in his trauma-scarred soul. It was a forgotten spark.

"Lower your weapon, human," Avotu's voice, deep and resonant, echoed from the vaults of the Observatory. There wasn't a drop of threat in it; it sounded muffled and incredibly tired. "If I wanted your death, you wouldn't even have time to inhale."
Alexander hesitated, but peering into the stranger's face, he caught the same brokenness he felt himself. He slowly lowered the crossbow.
"Who... what are you?" Alexander squeezed out of himself.

"I am the one who is already tired of burying those I couldn't protect," Avotu replied, and a yearning crimson reflection flashed in his black flame. The elf's vision instantly scanned their bodies. There was not a spark of natural mana in these people. They were absolutely empty, like vessels from another world. "You are from another dimension. You bear the trace of a spatial shift. You have no need to fear me. I only kill monsters, and you... you are still human."

Thunder roared deafeningly outside, making the floor shudder. The downpour pounded furiously on the ruins, threatening to flood the hall, and giant radioactive-driven scolopendras inevitably pulled toward them with the water.

Lightning flashed. Avotu smoothly, without aggression, drew a meteorite sword from its scabbard and drove it almost up to the hilt into the stone floor at the entrance. The Nature's Blade wailed, greedily absorbing the power of the storm thundering above them. The next moment, blinding blue lightning bolts tore from the black metal, weaving into a humming, tangible electric dome. The barrier cut off the ruins from the storm, acid rain, and blind creatures of the wasteland with a deafening crack. Inside the hall, it suddenly became quiet, dry, and absolutely safe.
The strangers opened their mouths in shock. This terrifying demon had just given them life, asking for nothing in return.

"You've come a long way to die in a puddle of ash," Avotu sat down on a fallen piece of a column, without taking his eyes off the mural on the wall. "I will shield you from the storm. Sit around the blade. And tell me what you hold on to each other for in this dead world."
